messages.content:
Simply summarize key insights from the below text at an 8th grade reading level excluding numbers: 0104: A clearer feedback processes for issues, we kind of assume that they’re known and they may not be as clear as we think they are. So if there was something that was impeding someone from participating or wanting to participate, how do they let us know besides just choosing not to?

0105: I think the main factor is our policies often set out processes and guides, and one of the big changes happening now and helping drive those change is early in those processes and guides, baking in those talks about accessibility. Rather than an afterthought it’s considered at or near the beginning and I think that that will have a big effect.

0106: Think that we need it actually in the policy. That would be the one biggest thing. It’s something we’ve identified that we want to do but I don’t think that it’s in place yet. Maybe there’s a fear that it’s going to slow us down or cause more work for us to figure it all out.

0107: Plain language and clear guidelines.

0112: Education, reduce process bureaucracy.

0115: To make them simpler, more concise, and to use plain language.

0116: I think defining the categories of differently abled people and having the physical and digital infrastructure around it. The most important is training people who are not differently abled to not treat those who are without bias. It starts with the mindset.

0117: We need ownership and involvement from persons with disabilities within the organization. The organization is 200 years old, there’s a lot of things built unconsciously, you need that different lens. It’s a gigantic undertaking, we try to deal with low-hanging fruit and build on those experiences. For a large organization we rely on executive support, that tone from the top helps at the grassroots level. We’re very mindful we don’t occupy other’s spaces, when there’s particular groups of employees that need support we grow that, work with our allies, and seek support. 

0118: Really strong focus on AODA, document remediation without focusing on how else accessibility impacts the procurement cycle. Right now the assessment of capacity is much greater than the actual capacity. Need to engage people with disabilities to understand more.

The text discusses the need for clearer feedback processes to address issues that may impede someone from participating. It suggests that policies should include talks about accessibility from the beginning, rather than as an afterthought. The importance of using plain language, clear guidelines, and reducing process bureaucracy is emphasized. The text also highlights the need to define categories of differently abled people and provide the necessary physical and digital infrastructure, as well as train people to treat those with disabilities without bias. It suggests involving people with disabilities within the organization to provide a different perspective and relying on executive support to promote accessibility at the grassroots level. The text mentions the focus on AODA (Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act) but also acknowledges the need to understand how accessibility impacts the procurement cycle and engage people with disabilities to gain more insights.
Now in point form and using acronyms

- Clear feedback processes are needed to address issues and obstacles to participation.
- Policies should include talks about accessibility from the beginning (ATB), rather than as an afterthought.
- Plain language and clear guidelines are important for accessibility.
- Education and reduction of process bureaucracy are necessary.
- Simplification, conciseness, and the use of plain language are important.
- Defining categories of differently abled people and establishing appropriate infrastructure is crucial.
- Training people without bias is essential.
- Ownership and involvement of persons with disabilities within the organization are needed.
- Executive support can help promote accessibility at the grassroots level.
- Collaboration with allies and seeking support is important.
- Focus on AODA (Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act) is key, but additional understanding is needed about accessibility's impact on procurement.
- Engaging people with disabilities can provide more insights.
identify supporting quotes with ID numbers

0105: "I think the main factor is our policies often set out processes and guides, and one of the big changes happening now and helping drive those change is early in those processes and guides, baking in those talks about accessibility. Rather than an afterthought it’s considered at or near the beginning and I think that that will have a big effect."

0112: "Education, reduce process bureaucracy."

0115: "To make them simpler, more concise, and to use plain language."

0116: "I think defining the categories of differently abled people and having the physical and digital infrastructure around it. The most important is training people who are not differently abled to not treat those who are without bias. It starts with the mindset."

0117: "We need ownership and involvement from persons with disabilities within the organization. The organization is 200 years old, there’s a lot of things built unconsciously, you need that different lens. It’s a gigantic undertaking, we try to deal with low-hanging fruit and build on those experiences. For a large organization we rely on executive support, that tone from the top helps at the grassroots level. We’re very mindful we don’t occupy other’s spaces, when there’s particular groups of employees that need support we grow that, work with our allies, and seek support."

0118: "Really strong focus on AODA, document remediation without focusing on how else accessibility impacts the procurement cycle. Right now the assessment of capacity is much greater than the actual capacity. Need to engage people with disabilities to understand more."
identify supporting quotes with ID numbers, use shortest form of each quote

0105: "Policies should include talks about accessibility from the beginning."
0112: "Education, reduce process bureaucracy."
0115: "To make them simpler, more concise, and to use plain language."
0116: "Define categories of differently abled people and train people without bias."
0117: "Ownership and involvement from persons with disabilities is needed."
0118: "Focus on AODA, understand how accessibility impacts procurement."
get ready for new task
Sure, I'm ready for the new task. Please let me know what you need assistance with and I'll do my best to help you.

In point form, simply summarize key insights from the below text at an 8th grade reading level and provide supporting quotes with ID numbers:
0106: We would like to do more small business work. Our challenge is we don’t always know about them. We are an enterprise and it’s challenging to find small businesses that are capable of the work, and that may be a perception thing that I have to watch for but it can often be a small business up against an Accenture or a Deloitte.
0112: The contracts are complicated and small companies just aren’t used to it. They don’t have the experience or advice and it becomes difficult and a truly unpleasant experience.
0114: It’s typically much larger companies. The smallest company that we worked with was still hundreds of employees. We did work with a small consultant (about 10 employees) through a tier-1 sourcing process.
0116: Yes on some occasions, but generally I work with larger suppliers.
0118: No specific priority for them although colloquially we know diverse businesses are small businesses typically
0122: I do have a few small domestic businesses within my team portfolio., and that’s where I see the struggle when I’m doing an RFP or trying to onboard them and sometimes I find they really have to look at the contract, they’ll ask to change it and we have to try to educate them on why we can’t just change the contract.
0125: I think competition should be open to anyone, but a typical component of RFPs is a financial component based on pricing, but I’ve seen it based on the financial stability of a company. You may not necessarily want someone who’s a 2 year starter without understanding more about their capabilities. I don’t think there’s a preference for large over small, but the stability component and references portion is important.
0126: Recently, in the last year, I see more small businesses. Before that it’s medium to large businesses. I’m not sure why the change or if there’s a preference for larger vendors. The total spend for small businesses I’m not sure, but I’d guess it was very small.
0129: They are all large suppliers unfortunately. We do go to public tender, and we do ask about diversity in the process. Some of these are in the smaller range closer to 100K, but my contracts are generally all are suppliers. There is work that could be done by smaller suppliers. Unless it’s under 100k we can’t go straight to a supplier, we have to do a competitive process.

- The organization wants to do more work with small businesses, but they face challenges in finding capable small businesses (0106).
- Small companies struggle with complicated contracts and lack the experience to navigate them (0112).
- The organization typically works with larger suppliers, but has worked with a smaller consultant in the past (0114, 0116).
- Diverse businesses, which often include small businesses, are not prioritized specifically, but they are acknowledged as being typically smaller (0118).
- The struggle with onboarding small businesses is seen when going through the RFP process and educating them on contract limitations (0122).
- When evaluating competition, financial stability and capabilities are considered, but there is no preference for large over small businesses (0125).
- There has been an increase in small businesses in recent years, but it's unclear why the change has occurred (0126).
- The organization primarily works with large suppliers, though there are opportunities for smaller suppliers for contracts under 100k (0129).

Supporting quotes with ID numbers:
0106: "We would like to do more small business work... it can often be a small business up against an Accenture or a Deloitte."
0112: "The contracts are complicated and small companies just aren’t used to it... a truly unpleasant experience."
0114: "It’s typically much larger companies... through a tier-1 sourcing process."
0116: "Yes on some occasions, but generally I work with larger suppliers."
0118: "No specific priority for them although colloquially we know diverse businesses are small businesses typically."
0122: "I see the struggle when I’m doing an RFP or trying to onboard them and sometimes I find they really have to look at the contract, they’ll ask to change it and we have to try to educate them on why we can’t just change the contract."
0125: "I don’t think there’s a preference for large over small... the stability component and references portion is important."
0126: "Recently, in the last year, I see more small businesses... I’d guess it was very small."
0129: "They are all large suppliers unfortunately... Unless it’s under 100k we can’t go straight to a supplier, we have to do a competitive process."
Now in point form, simply summarize those key insights at an 8th grade reading level without ID numbers

- The organization wants to work with small businesses, but it is challenging to find capable ones.
- Small companies struggle with complicated contracts and lack experience in navigating them.
- The organization typically works with larger suppliers, but has worked with a small consultant in the past.
- Diverse businesses, which often include small businesses, are not prioritized specifically, but they are acknowledged as typically smaller.
- Onboarding small businesses can be challenging due to contract limitations and the need for education.
- When evaluating competition, financial stability and capabilities are important, with no preference for large over small businesses.
- There has been an increase in small businesses in recent years, though the reason for this change is unclear.
- The organization primarily works with large suppliers, though there are opportunities for smaller suppliers for contracts under 100k.
Keep only points that support this fact: Procurement process design limits the ability to connect with small businesses or to have them compete fairly

- The organization finds it challenging to find small businesses capable of the work due to the procurement process design.
- Small companies struggle with complicated contracts, making it difficult for them to compete fairly in the procurement process.
- The organization primarily works with larger suppliers, limiting the ability of small businesses to connect and compete.
- Onboarding small businesses can be challenging due to contract limitations and the need for education.
- The organization acknowledges that diverse businesses, including small businesses, are typically smaller and may face limitations in the procurement process.

In point form, simply summarize key insights from the below text at an 8th grade reading level and provide supporting quotes with ID numbers:
0001: Lack of understanding. Lack of confidence. No corporate background. Living with Neurodivergence / disability (namely when diagnosed as an adult) teaches you not to trust your perception of yourself and others anad situations. Personal experience developed discomfort/distrust of corporate work and environments.
0006: Too new
0012: Looked at becoming pre-qualified for public contracts and was very laborious and difficult to fit into their criteria
0014: COVID, consultancy is a difficult sector because we’re an add-on. We did have a relationship building with a large organization, but they shifted their team around and the members we had a relationship with are no longer in their positions.
0017: A lot of red tape to do business with LPOs.
0018: The process is daunting
0019: The business has been around for 5 years and I’m the one of the only provider of these services in my geographic area, so people know to come here.

- Lack of understanding, confidence, and corporate background can create discomfort and distrust in corporate work environments (0001).
- Becoming pre-qualified for public contracts was laborious and difficult to fit into their criteria (0012).
- COVID has made consultancy a difficult sector, with relationship building being impacted by team restructuring in large organizations (0014).
- There is a lot of red tape involved in doing business with LPOs (0017).
- The process of doing business can be daunting (0018).
- The business has been established for 5 years and is one of the few providers of services in the geographic area, leading to recognition from customers (0019).

Supporting quotes with ID numbers:
0001: "Lack of understanding. Lack of confidence. No corporate background. Living with Neurodivergence / disability (namely when diagnosed as an adult) teaches you not to trust your perception of yourself and others and situations. Personal experience developed discomfort/distrust of corporate work and environments."
0012: "Looked at becoming pre-qualified for public contracts and was very laborious and difficult to fit into their criteria."
0014: "COVID, consultancy is a difficult sector because we’re an add-on. We did have a relationship building with a large organization, but they shifted their team around and the members we had a relationship with are no longer in their positions."
0017: "A lot of red tape to do business with LPOs."
0018: "The process is daunting."
0019: "The business has been around for 5 years and I’m the one of the only provider of these services in my geographic area, so people know to come here."
Now in point form, simply summarize those key insights at an 8th grade reading level without ID numbers

- Lack of understanding, confidence, and corporate background can create discomfort and distrust in corporate work environments.
- The process of becoming pre-qualified for public contracts is laborious and difficult to fit into the criteria.
- The consultancy sector has been impacted by COVID, with relationship building being affected by team restructuring in large organizations.
- There is a lot of red tape involved in doing business with LPOs.
- The process of doing business can be daunting.
- The business, established for 5 years, has gained recognition in its geographic area as one of the few providers of its services.
